beats.text: You just saw four rectangles and noticed differences between them. Things like how many rows they had or how long each row was. Now you're going to build your own rectangles on the grid, one row at a time. When you tap Check, you'll see how many squares you used. And we'll write a sentence that names what that area means. Everything we do today builds to one sentence: ___ is a multiple of ___ because ___ × ___ = ___. Let's build the first rectangle now.

incorrects.description: Student placed tiles but did not form a valid rectangle on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. A rectangle has rows of equal length.

incorrects.description: Student placed tiles but did not form a valid rectangle on second attempt

params.description: Grid shows example rectangle outline with 3 rows of 4 tiles each.

steps.text: Look at this example: 3 rows, 4 tiles in each row. Every row has the same number. Now use your 12 tiles to make rows that match.

incorrects.description: Student placed tiles but did not form a valid rectangle. System models a valid arrangement.

params.description: System arranges 12 tiles into 3 rows of 4, demonstrating equal row lengths.

steps.text: Let me show you. I'm placing 12 tiles in 3 rows. Each row has 4 tiles. Count them: row 1 has 4, row 2 has 4, row 3 has 4. When every row has the same number, that's a rectangle. The answer is 3 rows of 4.

steps.text: Check your numbers. Count the total tiles, then count the rows.

params.description: One row of the rectangle highlights.

steps.text: The first blank needs the total number of tiles you placed. The second blank needs how many rows you made. Count the rows on your rectangle now.

incorrects.description: Student filled slots incorrectly. System models the correct completion.

params.description: System fills both slots with values matching the student's built rectangle.

steps.text: Here's how to fill it. I count all the tiles: that's 12 total. I count the rows you made: that's the row count. The sentence is: My rectangle has 12 tiles in your-row-count rows. The total goes first, then the row count. That describes what you built.

steps.text: Count all the tiles in your rectangle. How many squares did you place?

incorrects.description: Student filled row_count slot incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Count the rows going across. Each row is one line of tiles.

incorrects.description: Student filled row_size slot incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Pick one row. Count how many squares are in that row.

steps.text: Count the highlighted rows. Each row is a separate line of tiles. How many rows do you see?

steps.text: Look at this highlighted row. Count the tiles in just this one row. That number tells you the size of each row.

params.description: Rows in the rectangle highlight sequentially.

steps.text: Let's break this down. First count all the tiles. Then count the rows. Then count how many tiles are in one row. The area equals the total tiles.

incorrects.description: Student filled one or more slots incorrectly after multiple attempts. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System animates counting all tiles, then highlights rows sequentially, then shows one row's tile count, then displays area label.

steps.text: Here's how to read this rectangle. Count all the tiles to get the total. Count the rows by looking at each horizontal line. Pick one row and count its tiles to find the row size. The area equals the total number of tiles because area measures how many square units fill the shape. Now fill in the sentence using those four values.

params.description: Student's 12-tile rectangle animates smoothly from free-placed positions to grid-aligned coordinates. Each tile moves to its nearest grid cell. Ambiguous overlaps resolve to one tile per cell; extras removed with undo affordance visible. Grid snap toggles ON.

beats.text: Watch what happens to your rectangle. It's snapping into the grid. Same rectangle, easier to read.

elements.description: Hundred Grid in Mode B with grid snap ON. 12 tiles arranged in a rectangle, now aligned to grid coordinates. Numeric labels hidden. Side handles not exposed.

elements.template: ___ tiles fill ___ rows of ___, so the area is ___ square units

incorrects.description: Student filled slots incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Look at the area label on your rectangle. That number goes in the first slot.

incorrects.description: Student filled slots incorrectly on second attempt

params.description: Dimension labels and area label highlight on the rectangle.

steps.text: Look at the labels on your rectangle. The area goes in slot 1. The fixed side length, 4, goes in slot 2. Then width times height equals that area.

incorrects.description: Student filled slots incorrectly three or more times. System models the correct completion.

params.description: System fills all slots in sequence: area value in slot 1, fixed side 4 in slot 2, width and height in slots 3-4-5 matching the multiplication.

steps.text: The area is a multiple of 4 because when you multiply 4 times the number of rows, you get the area. That is what the sentence names. The area has to be a multiple of 4 because each row adds 4 more squares.

incorrects.description: Student filled one or more slots incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Look at the labels on your rectangle. Which number shows the total area?

incorrects.description: Student filled one or more slots incorrectly on second attempt

params.description: Area label at rectangle center highlights.

steps.text: The area goes in the first blank. The fixed side length, 4, goes in the second blank. Then fill the multiplication to show width times height equals that area.

incorrects.description: Student filled one or more slots incorrectly three or more times. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System fills all five slots in order: area value in slot 1, 4 in slot 2, width in slot 3, height in slot 4, area in slot 5.

steps.text: Here's how this sentence works. The area is a multiple of 4 because 4 times the number of rows equals the area. The first blank names the area. The second blank names what it's a multiple of. The last three blanks show the multiplication that proves it.
